text:
Recently came upon a channel based on the topic of electroplating 3D printed, faux jewelry. It’s quite clever and seemingly easy to do. Uses a conductive spray paint as a base coat on the prints, then uses various plating techniques.
Your project seems like it would gain some efficiency by using silver plated (for example) parts in lieu of taping on sheets of aluminum. Don’t get me wrong, your build is awesome as it sits. I look forward to the next one!!

author: @TonyMoffe
text: And for the final thought, I think progress and technology is why we can’t figure this out for instance radio receivers when they were first invented, all relied on tubes to amplify the injected frequency by varying the grid voltage. Every tube has its own characteristics, but their original purpose was to amplify Very weak, electromagnetic or electrical signals to be much stronger and it all works on the basis of the potential difference of the positive and negative with tubes I guess even with transistors it is possible to create a lower than zero negative potential and at the same time a much higher than positive potential More or less doubling your power without any extra input so a multiple stage two amplifier could raise the 2 V with a couple of different antennas not just one to a usable point to run a high frequency high voltage circuit to create enough static energy to do some real work with an atmospheric or electrostatic motor. My research on Tesla says to me in my mind, the Tesla coil was never invented to generate electricity. I believe what he was doing, was perfecting his version of the radio, and when Marconi got all the patents, he then shifted his research to electricity With his current set up setting systems. He was able to amplify atmospheric energy to an insane amount and I believe that we’re only limited because of current technology current electronic components don’t sync up as well or allow us to put systems in the correct resident frequency to operate efficiently. My belief is that residence is key to efficiency and rather than trying to add a bridge rectifier and turning the AC power coming in to DC it would be better to add a tuning circuit such as a crystal radio tuner that is in residence with the incoming power or at least at a harmonic of that residence to increase the efficiency and the usage of bandwidth to make the system more efficient and usable. I believe that this is what Tesla discovered. The incoming energy is at many many many different frequencies and tuning a circuit to allow the widest bandwidth in the highest energy zone was the key.

author: @TonyMoffe
text: Also, I wonder if after having watched your videos about atmospheric energy where even with an antenna a couple of feet high, you were able to create a couple of volts AC so too create the high-voltage static electricity couldn’t we just run that low-voltage electricity through a high frequency, resident transformer And a circuit similar to I don’t know maybe the slayer exciter circuit or a dual thief circuit to get the initial step up before the transformer I don’t think we need to go several hundred feet in the air to get a high voltage. We could absolutely create that ourselveselectrostatic motors. I don’t believe are as dependent on amperage as they are voltage.

author: @TonyMoffe
text: I’d like to start off by saying I love your channel. I’ve been subscribed to it for. I’m not even sure eight years probably under a different username. I love that most everything you do is free and open source. I’ve learned so much from your channel. Also, I don’t know if you realize but your channel is so much more than just informative. You’ve really changed my views on a lot of things education and knowledge the willingness to unselfishly pass on that knowledge growing up, I hated school, which made me always feel like I didn’t want or didn’t have the time to learn you have changed my perspective on that. You literally have made me want to be a better person. I thank you for That Now the point of the comment is this my understanding of an atmospheric motor is that it works very similar to an AC induction motor a positive and negative field is introduced to the rotor, whether it be magnetic or static it reacts to the same way, so would it be true that the atmospheric motor would become stronger and more efficient with more mass in the rotor instead of using just aluminum tape, I believe that a heavier duty rotor not one solid chunk of aluminum, but maybe an aluminum pipe would have a much stronger reaction to the force is applied to it creating a more powerful motor. I don’t have the means or money to do a lot of experiments on my own. I’m just introducing a thought that I have I may or may not be right and I believe you’re the man to introduce that thought too lol thank you for your channel. Thank you for all that you do. I appreciate you.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I was doing some consultation for a company who were working with this, but they ran into some issues that I can't say because of an NDA, but I CAN say it was NOT because the theory did not work.

I've been working ways around the problems they experienced, and hopefully will try to perfect the solution in a way that I can share with them for interest in their company in return.

If it works, I'll have all the money I need to go on to experiment with a whole lot of other cool inventions.

I may never get rich, but I'm not worried about getting rich, just having enough to afford to go about tinkering.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
I have lasersaber's otiginal print files for his large atmo motor. I made the sliding adjustment peices out of TPU. They slide easily but with enough grip to stay put.  Not sure if the metal teirdrop tubes he used would be better than Rob's 3d printer and tape tubes, but Robs allow for easy ways to try different materials, like coper tape ir other exotic materials not available in the tear drop tunes.  

Another advantage is you don't rho dog

author: @kevinleebailey
text:
Some atmospheric charge is produced by cleavage of water droplets evaporating from a body of water that is grounded.
I found this bit of knowledge in an old electrical engineering book (1860s or 1870s ?) I purchased at a carboot sale.
It's a bit like Lord Kelvin's thunderstorm in reverse.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text:
The Sun is like a gigantic plasma ball radiating positive charge (proton's)(cosmic wind). This is also where some atmospheric charge comes from. 
It also explains why there is a hole in the ozone layer above the South Pole.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Awesome, as always.

I've had a mad thought that's troubled my dreams a few times for harnessing atmospheric energy-  a wimshurst tower.  

I have no notion if such a thing would work or even how to go about it, or if it would be worth the effort, but it seemed like you might be able to stairstep the charge difference between offset disks or something, between ground level and some way in the air.  In looking at how the wimshurst worked, and then the atmospheric notion, Something is pinging around my brain but I can't quite get there.  
If that inspires anybody to take a crack at it, you're more than welcome to it.

author: @Unpopular_0pinion
text: Love it, Robert! Machines like these would be useful for so many remote applications. Thank you for taking the time to share :) I do have one question.. Is there a way to make the ground more attractive for the electrons moving down the wire? Might be a uneducated question with a simple answer. You can put the wire higher to access higher voltage but the ground remains the same ground. My intuition tells me digging a deep hole wouldn't necessarily create a more grounded ground. But I guess my question is, could you use a negative charge instead of a ground to turn the motor? Or is the ground already most favorable?

author: @GabrielPettier
text:
A drone might not be necessary, a kite would probably do the trick as well, these can go quite high and once they are up, they are pretty stable, as a bonus the up and down movements generated by even a slight wind could also be used to generate electricity :), i need to test something like that some day.

I wonder if, using enough of such motors, we could actually prevent important tension buildup in the atmosphere, and thus, the kind of storm they cause.

replies.author: @ABaumstumpf
replies.text:
"the kind of storm they cause."
You got cause and effect backwards.

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text: @ABaumstumpf Hm, i was thinking about thunder, but i see your point, tension is the cause of thunder, but it's the consequence of the rest.

author: @walterbaltzley4546
text:
The key to tapping atmospheric power is to look at the generator as a "charge pump".  There is enormous electrical potential in the atmosphere, but that that potential is on a continuous GRADIENT so that you rarely get a massive discharge from sky to ground.  To get the charge to FLOW, you need to remove the gradient and create a conductive channel without any electrical charge.  The trick to achieving this can be found inside the VANDEGRAAFF machine.  

NOTE that we are NOT interested in the high-voltages that can be achieved on the OUTSIDE of device, but rather the NEUTRAL CHARGE on the INSIDE of the sphere.  If this were made into an elongated vertical shape, then INSIDE we could generate a current between the high-voltage potential top, and the lower potential at its base.  We could use that current to power a circuit inside the neutral zone.

Thus, the Vandegraaff Machine could operate as a pump to generate a kind of electrostatic vacuum pressure, allowing charge to flow between the high and low potential in the atmosphere.

author: @Robert-y2k7s
text:
If you had a Mylar weather balloon tethered, say, +/- 300m or 1000’ up, with three Kevlar threads to stabilize the balloon over one location, and connected the motor with a hairlike filament of tungsten wire the weight of the tethering and the electrical connection might not be so much as to be problematic. 

Though, in the United States the FAA might make some objection. No doubt the lighting they would require to alert air traffic would obviate the effectiveness of the electrical gathering.

replies.author: @RichardElniski
replies.text:
Yes, this is due able up to 500 ft. or the max height of a non-commercial drone. Start with a 
triangle frame shape, crisscrossed with nylon fishing line to which Teflon tape streamers are
attached. Coat the fishing line with an epoxy copper power mixture. Suspend the frame by 
tethered Mylar Helium balloons attached with wire to the frame. Now you have a simple cheap
Positive Charge collector. Attach the Tethering lines to a plasma arc Hydrogen Generator and
collect the gas for later use.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
I think mylar coated plastics are the lightest, for strength per weight.
And would also retain light fluids that really want to escape, like hydrogen, better than usual plastics.

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: @yasirrakhurrafat1142  NightHawkInLight made some good experiments on hot-air balloons. I think it needs to be quite big to be able to lift even thin copper.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
@Kangsteri  that's why I'm saying.
Hydrogen would fair much better.
Mylar would contain hydrogen well.
And wouldn't be too heavy.
By hot air, you mean he likely used regular air right?
Electrolysis isn't too difficult. To get hydrogen easily.
And if you make your balloon unnecessarily big, small breezes would sway it wildly.
I look him up regularly too though, guy's an absolute genius.
He's able to actually replicate methods and cryptic science journals, and sometimes come up with more accessible, available and convenient methods to achieve pretty close results, with authentic ideas for application of those contraptions.
Love him!
Also, our guy here Robert Murray Smith! Love him too!

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: @yasirrakhurrafat1142 Sky lantern might be one cheap and easy option too.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
@Kangsteri  could be considerable.. but thinking it through.. it'll use more energy than gained by the static potential.
The harvest will be negative.
Good for experimenting though.
I think a rather better idea would be to triple anchor 3 ropes in a triangle position (corners), and send up a turbine up there, hanging from a relatively large blimp, the wind will do a better job of harvesting energy.
Maybe Don't even send a turbine up there, just connect a rope and let movements of a balloon drive the rope on the ground.. harvesting energy while it does.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering prior to this comment I responded twice, but only one of them is visible unless the 'Sort by' drop-down menu on the comment section is set to 'Newest first'.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It can be hard for me to find comments mate - you have to remember i have thousands of them and get about 3-400 new ones every day - I basically do my best and often it's not good enough. Anyway - I hate to say this but I don't really know what you are trying to achieve - I get the idea in general and I suppose you can't fit them in the physical space of your e-scooter which is why you are concerned with their layout - also when you say disposable vapes - what size battery have they? My understanding is there is a variety of size and it is a bad idea to mix sizes - so stick to 18650s and use standard boards - if you have to remodel the space - do that - there is not a lot else to say really
